source: All-In Podcast — Trump transition, SEC/crypto regulation, xAI Colossus, AI scaling laws, MicroStrategy, defense tech
summary: Nuclear energy is the best near-term solution for US electricity capacity expansion; solar is the long-term winner but 50 years away from dominance
summary_extended: Baker argues that nuclear energy is the best near-term solution for US electricity capacity expansion, citing its environmental credentials (when done correctly) and immediate availability as key advantages over alternatives. He frames this in the context of AI datacenter buildout and US manufacturing competitiveness, where power constraints are an acute bottleneck that cannot wait for longer-term solutions. Solar, he contends, will ultimately dominate global energy production through compounding efficiency gains in photovoltaics and battery storage, but remains land-constrained and is roughly 50 years away from being cheap enough to broadly displace coal. Baker's view is that both nuclear and solar are needed, but nuclear is the actionable near-term investment thesis while solar is a multi-decade structural story. No specific companies or price targets were cited, leaving the thesis at the thematic/sector level without a named equity expression.
detail: Baker endorses nuclear as environmentally friendly, available now, and critical for AI/manufacturing competitiveness. Solar will eventually dominate via compounding efficiency gains in photovoltaics and batteries but is land-constrained and decades away from being cheap enough to displace coal broadly. Both are needed.
confidence_evidence: Baker says 'more nuclear more better' and 'I think it is highly likely that in my lifetime the world just runs on solar' — clear directional views with reasoning
quote_fragment: nuclear is arguably just as environmentally friendly done right and it is here now
